content: Tuschl's Motif Pattern: To design RNA molecules using Tuschl's rules, select the option button next to Tuschl's Motif Pattern and then select one or more pattern checkboxes. The Designer will generate 19-nucleotide RNA sequences that match the selected pattern(s). For example, if you select the AA(N19)TT pattern, the Designer will search for an area of the sequence that begins with "AA" and ends with "TT," with 19 bases in between. Each pattern is listed using the following base code letters: A = Adenine; T = Thymine; R = Adenine or Guanine (Purines); Y = Thymine or Cytosine (Pyrimidines); N = Any
section: Step 2. This algorithm performs a statistical analysis of the target sequence—considering such factors as sequence composition, nucleotide content at 5' and 3' ends, and thermodynamic properties—and correlates it with data collected from multiple validated siRNA oligo sets tested on different gene targets.

content:
Overview
Short-hairpin RNA (shRNA) is an RNA molecule that contains sense and antisense sequences connected by a short spacer of nucleotides that enables the molecule to form a loop structure. shRNA is expressed in mammalian cells from a vector with a Pol III-type promoter. It is transported from the nucleus into the cytoplasm, where it's processed by the Dicer enzyme into 21-23 nucleotide siRNA duplexes. Once in the cell, the shRNA can enter the RNAi pathway and result in gene silencing by blocking translation.
section: shRNA - Enter Sequence and Parameters / Default Motif Pattern: Select the option button next to Default Motif Pattern to use Invitrogen's proprietary design algorithm. This algorithm performs a statistical analysis of the target sequence—considering such factors as sequence composition, nucleotide content at 5' and 3' ends, and thermodynamic properties—and correlates it with data collected from multiple validated siRNA oligo sets tested on different gene targets.

content:
Overview
MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are endogenously expressed small ssRNA sequences of ~22 nucleotides in length that naturally direct gene silencing through components shared with the RNAi pathway. BLOCK-iT™ Pol II miR RNAi Technology uses expression vectors that allow flexible expression of miRNA-based knockdown cassettes driven by RNA Polymerase II (Pol II) promoters in mammalian cells.
To generate miR RNAi oligos for use with this system, you can use BLOCK-iT™ RNAi Designer to design two single-stranded DNA oligonucleotides: one encoding the target pre-miRNA (top-strand oligo) and the other its complement (bottom-strand oligo).
section: miR RNAi - Enter Sequence and Parameters / Step 5: Select Vector and Strand Orientation and click "RNAi Design" to Design.

content:
The top-strand oligo encoding the target pre-miRNA has the following features:
·A 4-nucleotide, 5' overhang (TGCT) complementary to the vector (required for directional cloning)
·A 5' G + a short 21-nucleotide antisense sequence (mature miRNA) derived from the target gene, followed by
·A short spacer of 19 nucleotides to form a terminal loop and
·A short sense target sequence with 2 nucleotides removed to create an internal loop
See the BLOCK-iT™ Pol II miR RNAi Expression Vector Kits manual for more information, including details about the oligo design rules used by the RNAi Designer.

Select one of the predesigned 4-nucleotide loop sequences from the Default Loop Sequence pulldown list, or enter a custom loop sequence of 4–11 nucleotides in the Custom Loop Sequence field.

Select either the pENTR™/U6 entry vector for constitutive shRNA expression or the pENTR™/H1/TO entry vector for inducible expression. Note that if you select pENTR™ /U6, the shRNA oligo designs will be compatible with both vectors.

content:
High Predictive Accuracy with Interpretability: As one of the first tools using rigorous machine learning (LASSO regression) on a large dataset, it matched or surpassed the performance of contemporary "black-box" neural networks (BIOPREDsi). Its linear model provides clear, quantitative insights into which sequence features promote or hinder efficacy, a significant advantage for mechanistic understanding over opaque models.
Comprehensive Off-Target Analysis Suite: Goes beyond simple homology filtering by integrating SCF calculation, an early and methodologically sound approach to predict miRNA-like off-target effects based on seed region complementarity to 3'UTRs, a feature not uniformly present in earlier tools.
Open Access & Transparent Methodology: The algorithm, based on a peer-reviewed, publicly described linear model, is fully transparent. The web server has been freely available, and the approach does not rely on proprietary or undisclosed rules, allowing for independent evaluation and reproducibility.
section: DSIR (Designer of Small Interfering RNA) - Raw Article Summary / Advantages:
parent_content:
Tool Name: DSIR (Designer of Small Interfering RNA)
Developer/Unit: Jean-Philippe Vert, Nicolas Foveau, Christian Lajaunie, Yves Vandenbrouck (Centre for Computational Biology, Ecole des Mines de Paris & CEA Grenoble, France)
Core Principles:
·Employs a linear regression model trained with LASSO on a large, unbiased siRNA dataset (Huesken et al., 2005), achieving state-of-the-art prediction accuracy (Pearson correlation ~0.67).
·Utilizes a composite feature representation combining: 1) Sparse representation (nucleotide identity at each specific position, including the 3' overhang), and 2) Spectral representation (frequency of short sequence motifs of length 1-3, capturing position-independent compositional biases).
·The model is inherently interpretable; the learned weights quantify the positive or negative contribution of each nucleotide at each position and each short motif to the predicted silencing efficacy.
·Integrates specificity filters including BLAST-based similarity search against RefSeq databases and Seed Complement Frequency (SCF) calculation to estimate potential off-target effects via the miRNA-like seed region (nt 2-8 of the guide strand).

content:
Primarily Sequence-Based, Lacks Target Structure Consideration: The core model focuses exclusively on siRNA sequence features. It does not incorporate target mRNA secondary structure or accessibility calculations, a factor recognized as important and integrated into more biophysically-oriented tools like OligoWalk.
Model Trained on Early Dataset: Its performance is benchmarked on the 2005 Huesken dataset. While robust, later tools may benefit from larger, more diverse training datasets and incorporate subsequent discoveries in RNAi biology that are not captured in this model.
No Specialized Design for Advanced Formats: The tool is designed for standard 19-21nt siRNA duplexes. It does not offer dedicated design modules for newer formats like Dicer-substrate RNAs (DsiRNAs), which are optimized in vendor tools like IDT's, or for shRNA vector design, which is a strength of tools like siRNA Wizard.
Computational Interface May Be Dated: As a legacy academic web server, its user interface and processing speed may be less streamlined or modern compared to contemporary commercial or actively-maintained academic platforms.

section: DSIR (Designer of Small Interfering RNA) - Raw Article Summary

content:
1. Sequence definition
This part requires a name for your DSIR session in order to keep track.
Sequence name
Enter a name for your design session to identify it precisely (i.e. my_targetname_design_1). This
field is mandatory, a message will informed you in case of omission.
Sequence
Paste your target sequence in FASTA format or click on Browse button to upload your sequence
file from your computer (use a file in fasta format).
FASTA format only : your sequence must respect FASTA format.
If your sequence is in an incorrect format, DSIR will inform you by an error and ask you to check
your sequence.
section: DSIR User Guide / Limitations:

content: The first step of the design performed by DSIR relies on the prediction of the efficacy of all possible siRNAs sequence targeting your input sequence. DSIR computes this efficacy thanks to the weights associated with some features (a model that combine a specific nucleotide at a given position and nucleotides motifs) of the sequence, for a detailed description of the model please read: An accurate and interpretable model for siRNA efficacy prediction (Vert, J-P., Foveau, N., Lajaunie, C., Vandenbrouck, Y., BMC Bioinformatics, 2006 7:520).
section: DSIR User Guide / 2. Efficacy prediction settings

content:
Depending on your needs, three models are currently available:
·19nt means a design based on the linear model for 19 nt without 2 nt overhangs that can be further added manually for synthesis (i.e. with symmetric 3'TT overhangs).
·21nt means a design based on the linear model for 21 nt with 2 nt overhangs belonging to the target sequence.
·shRNA means a design based on the linear model for 19 nt with the cloning site (forward and reverse) and internal loop sequences included during the export of the final results.
section: DSIR User Guide / RNA design

content:
During efficacy prediction, each siRNAs sequences are ranked by their percentage of efficacy. You
can adjust the number of displayed siRNA sequences by modifying the "Efficacy threshold" field.
Only siRNAs with a predicted efficacy greater than the threshold value are kept and displayed. By
default this value is set to 90.
section: DSIR User Guide / Score threshold

content:
siRNA sequences harbouring polynucleotides tracts (i.e 4 occurences of "A" or more) in their content have been shown to lower target gene silencing. This option allows to detect and to filter all siRNA sequences that contains polyN tracts (with N >= 4). By default this option is set to "Yes".
Avoid immunostimulatory motifs
Toll-like receptors (TLRs) expressed in endosomes have been reported to recognize single- and double-stranded siRNA eliciting an interferon response. These TLRs serve as pattern-recognition sensors of specific immunostimulatroy motifs (such as 5'-UGUGU-3' or 5'-GUCCUUCAA-3' in the siRNA guide strand) that should be avoided in siRNA design [Hornung et al., Nat. Med., 2005;Judge et al., Nat. Biotech., 2005]. This option allows to detect and to filter all siRNA sequences that contains these motifs. By default this option is set to "Yes".
section: DSIR User Guide / Avoid 4 or more nucleotides runs

content: siRNA design and efficacy prediction results are reported in this part. Each column can be ranked by clicking on its header. Each siRNA can be selected either for further analysis (i.e. potential offtarget search, see section 4) or for export into formatted file (see section 5).
section: 3. Results / Avoid 4 or more nucleotides runs

content:
·"siRNA_id": identifier attributed to each siRNA numbered from the highest DSIR score to the lowest.
·"Pos.": position site where the siRNA guide strand begins in the target sequence (from 5' to 3').
·"SS sequence": sense strand (passenger strand) siRNA sequence from 5' to 3'.
· "AS sequence": antisense strand (guide strand) siRNA sequence from 5' to 3'.
·"Score": predicted efficacy according to the selected model (19 or 21nt).
·"Corrected Score": previous efficacy score minored by the penalties from some intrinsic target features which have been shown to influence siRNA efficacy (Filhol et al., PLoS One, 2012 7:10).
section: 3. Results / Columns for efficacy prediction:

content:
·"OT": number of potential off-targets (namely the number of hits with the chosen mismatch tolerance) found in the selected databanks.
·"SCF Hits": number of SCF (Seed Complement Frequencies) or hits in a 3' UTR transcript databank. The higher this value is the more off-target effects are suspected.
·"#Seqs": number of 3'UTR sequences matched.
·"1 Hit": number of seeds matching a 3'UTR sequence only one time.
·"2 Hits": number of seeds matching the same 3'UTR sequence two times.
·">=3 Hits": number of seeds matching the same 3'UTR sequence three times or more. The higher this value is, the more off-target effect through miRNA pathway is probable.
section: 3. Results / Columns for siRNA similarity search:

content: To check how specific your siRNA guide strand sequence, an exact similarity search can be performed with the siRNAs set that satisfy the efficacy threshold. This can be done either by screening mRNA sequence databank for potential off-targets according to a given mismatchtolerance or by computing the seed complement frequencies (SCF) in order to check the complementarity between the seed region of the siRNA guide strand and the 3'UTR of the offtargeted genes.
section: 4. Similarity search / Columns for siRNA similarity search:

content: From the siRNA sequence set, you can perform the similarity search either on all siRNAs satisfying the efficacy threshold or select a subset you want to analyze. For a subset, select rows for each sequence of interest. No selection means that all siRNA sequences will be submitted. To run the similarity click on the "Search bank" button at the end of the page.
section: 4. Similarity search / Selection

content: The algorithm of similarity search used by DSIR is based on exact pattern matching and allow mismatches. You can set this mismatch tolerance which represents the number of allowed mismatch between your siRNA sequence and sequences from the databank (set 0 if you want a total exact similarity search). By default this value is set to 1. Although the search algorithm is linear with the sequence databank space, you should keep in mind that the computing time increase with this value.
section: 4. Similarity search / Mismatch tolerance

content: You can choose the bank against which you want to check the specificity. In the current release of DSIR, six databanks are available built from the RefSeq NCBI section and comprise three organisms (Homo sapiens, Mus musculus, Rattus norvegicus and a concatenation of these species for cross-species detection). These databanks are updated daily.
section: 4. Similarity search / Data bank

content: It has been reported that complementarity between the 3'UTR regions of the off-targeted gene and the seed region of the siRNA guide strand is critical for off-targeting and that SCF can be used as a predictor for siRNA specificity [Birmingham et al., Nat. Met., 2006]. Select this option if you want to compute SCF. The length of the seed region of the siRNA guide strand can comprises nucleotides 2-7 or 2-8. By default the length of the seed region is set to 7 (position 2-8 nt). To run the similarity search algorithm, click on the "Search Bank" button.
section: 4. Similarity search / Compute SCF (Seed Complement Frequencies)

content: This option allows to filter the siRNA sequences according to the number of potential off-targets sequence. A value set to 1 means that all siRNA sequences matching more than one sequence (normally the proper target gene from which the siRNA is designed for) in the sequence space search are removed from the results page avoiding siRNA sequence that presents hit to unintended sequence.
section: 4. Similarity search / Filter by off-target

content:
The results of your DSIR session can be exported by selecting your favorite file format to send
your orders to the manufacturers. The supported file format are: csv (for Excel sheet), text and
xml.
section: 4. Similarity search / 5. Export results

content:
·siRNA Target Finder
Uses ΔG1 (internal stability of siRNA duplex) based on pentamer free energy to filter candidates.
Employs ΔG2 (specificity score) calculated from binding strength differences to target vs. non - target mRNAs .
Filters based on secondary structure and removes low - complexity sequences.
Checks for SNPs to avoid disruption of silencing.
·siRNA Construct Builder
Designs hairpin inserts for vectors/cassettes with customizable loop sequences and transcription start site optimization .
·siRNA Sequence Scrambler
Generates scrambled control sequences with same nucleotide composition, filtered via BLAST against RefSeq.

Developer/Unit：Luquan Wang and Forest Y. Mu (GenScript Corporation)
section: Genscript siRNA Target Finder - Raw Article Summary

content:
Machine learning algorithm
Based on a large number of experimental data of RNAi and advanced data mining technology, GenScript now develops a newest machine learning algorithm to predict siRNA. Compared with our first generation design tool, the accuracy of new algorithm, especially for the design of shRNA, has greatly enhanced by 90%. The siRNA design algorithm developed by GenScript, along with its siRNA Genome-Wide Bio-Reagent Bank (GenPoolTM) library, represents a major improvement over our previous design approach.
section: siRNA Target Finding Strategy[1] / Limitations：

content:
To maximize the prediction accuracy of RNAi design, GenScript scientists incorporate the following empirical rules into the novel generation RNAi design software successfully:
1. Thermodynamic property: Sequences were filtered to remove candidates with unfavored thermodynamic property [2, 4, 5] .
2. Length of the siRNA target site: Default is 21 mer [3] .
3. GC content of the target site: Default GC content range is 30-60%. Sequences with low GC content (< 60%) have a better chance to be functional siRNAs site than those with high GC content (> 60%). For GC rich genes, the upper limit of the GC content range may be adjusted to a higher level.
4. Sequence region: We recommend ORF (open reading frame) as the target selecting region, preferably 50–100 nt downstream of the "ATG" start codon, and you may specify your desired region in our design tool.
5. Organism: The human, mouse, and rat genomes are currently available. The specified genome is used to remove non-unique target sequences via BLAST/SmithWaterman search.
section: siRNA Target Finding Strategy[1] / Empirical rules

content:
7. Repeat and low complex sequence: Sequences with tandem repeats and other low complex features will be removed.
8. Filtering sequences: Filtering sequences are the sequences that trigger mammalian immune response [6, 7] .
9. SNP: Sequences overlapping with a SNP site will be marked and filtered. This is done only when an accession/gene id is provided.
10. ΔE and ranking: The candidates are ranked based on a GenScript proprietary algorithm and using the parameter ΔE. This parameter measures the specificity of a target site.
11. Positive motif: Positive motifs is a group of motifs which can significantly influence the in vivo application of siRNA owing to off target effects and toxicities associated with innate immunte stimulation. Therefore, siRNA containing positive motifs will be suppressed; in the other hand, siRNA containing negative motifs will be given a bonus.
section: siRNA Target Finding Strategy[1] / 6. RNA secondary structure: We calculated the RNA secondary structure and minimum free energy (MFE) for each target sense and anti-sense sequences. Sequences with relatively strong internal structures will be removed.

content:
Off-target of RNAi operation is an important feature of GenScript RNAi design software, which stands for the latest developing trend of RNAi technology and application. Controlling off-target efficiency is performed:
1. BLAST/SmithWaterman search:
The imperfectly matched target mRNA with siRNA is a major factor for occurrence of off-target unintended genes. Therefore, each candidate target sequence will be searched against a unique and comprehensive EST/mRNA collection for the specified organism. Sequences overlapping 16 bp or more with other transcripts are removed from the final list.
section: Controlled off-target efficiency / 6. RNA secondary structure: We calculated the RNA secondary structure and minimum free energy (MFE) for each target sense and anti-sense sequences. Sequences with relatively strong internal structures will be removed.

content:
2.1 Seed region: Previous investigations and GenScript experiment data show that siRNA "off target" transcript silencing mediated by seed region sequence is widespread [8] ."Seed region" of siRNA is analogous to seed region of microRNA. GenScript researchers found that size of seed region plays a major part in off target prediction. Size of seed region of siRNA we proposed is 6-8 bases.
2.2. Seed match: Depending on the proprietary off-target prediction database, threshold of seed match for the specific species in siRNA design is a unique characteristic in GenScript’s siRNA design software. This cutoff value is computed by a probability model which has general biological significance.
section: Controlled off-target efficiency / 2. Off-target of seed match of siRNA:

content: Besides general sequence alignment, GenScript siRNA design tool incorporates a novel alignment approach, functional alignment. This idea for functional alignment derives from asymmetry of siRNA in the assembly of the RNAi enzyme complex.
section: Controlled off-target efficiency / 3. Functional alignment:

content: GenScript guarantees that 95% of off target effect will be filtered if all filtering conditions are chosen. However, some candidate siRNAs with highest knockdown efficiency are filtered owing to carry out a very strict screening condition. Therefore, we should make a tradeoff between off-target effect and gene knockdown efficiency.
section: Controlled off-target efficiency / Announcement:

content:
1. Wang L, Mu FY. A Web-based design center for vector-based siRNA and siRNA cassette. Bioinformatics. 2004; 20(11): 1818–1820.
2. Khvorova A, Reynolds A, Jayasena SD. Functional siRNAs and miRNAs exhibit strand bias. Cell. 2003; 115(2): 209–216.
3. Harborth J, Elbashir SM, Vandenburgh K, et al. Sequence, chemical, and structural variation of small interfering RNAs and short hairpin RNAs and the effect on mammalian gene silencing. Antisense Nucleic Acid Drug Dev. 2003; 13(2): 83–105.
4. Reynolds A, Leake D, Boese Q, et al. Rational siRNA design for RNA interference. Nat Biotechnol. 2004; 22(3): 326–330.
5. Ui-Tei K, Naito Y, Takahashi F, et al. Guidelines for the selection of highly effective siRNA sequences for mammalian and chick RNA interference. Nucleic Acids Res. 2004; 32(3): 936–948.
6. Adam DJ, Vandana S, Janet RS, et al. Sequence-dependent stimulation of the mammalian innate immune response by synthetic siRNA. Nat Biotechnology. 2005; 23: 457–462
7. Hornung V, Guenthner-Biller M, Bourquin C, et al. Sequence-specific potent induction of IFN-alpha by short interfering RNA in plasmacytoid dendritic cells through TLR7. Nat Medicine. 2005; 11(3): 263–270
8. Jackson LA, Burchard J, Schelter J, et al. Widespread siRNA ‘‘off-target’’ transcript silencing mediated by seed region sequence complementarity. RNA. 2006; 12(7): 1179–1187.
section: References: / Announcement:

content:
·Thermodynamic Hybridization Model:
The core algorithm rigorously calculates the free energy changes (ΔG°) for siRNA binding to its target mRNA. It specifically accounts for the energy costs of disrupting both the target mRNA's secondary structure and the siRNA's own intramolecular and intermolecular structures, modeling the full equilibrium between all these states.
·Partition Function Calculation:
Employs a partition function method to consider the ensemble of all possible secondary structures for both siRNA and target mRNA, providing a more comprehensive thermodynamic profile than methods relying on a single minimum free energy structure.
·Machine Learning Integration:
A Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier integrates the calculated thermodynamic parameters with known siRNA sequence features (e.g., GC content, terminal asymmetry) to predict the probability that a candidate siRNA will achieve high silencing efficacy (>70% inhibition).
·Empirical Rule Pre-filter:
Optionally uses established empirical rules (e.g., Reynolds score) to pre-screen and filter out unpromising candidates before the computationally intensive thermodynamic calculations, significantly speeding up the process.
section: OligoWalk - Raw Article Summary / Core Principles:
parent_content:
Tool Name: OligoWalk
Developer/Unit: Zhi John Lu and David H. Mathews (University of Rochester Medical Center, USA)

content:
Target Accessibility Focus: Uniquely emphasizes and rigorously calculates the thermodynamic accessibility of the target mRNA binding site, a critical factor often oversimplified or ignored by other tools that rely primarily on siRNA sequence features alone.
Comprehensive Thermodynamic Model: Uses a more complete and physically realistic model of the binding equilibrium, considering the structure of both interacting partners, which can improve prediction accuracy for challenging targets.
High Predictive Value: Reports a high positive predictive value of 78.6%, meaning nearly 80% of its top-ranked siRNA candidates are expected to be highly effective (>70% silencing) in experiments.
Transparency and Flexibility: Provides detailed thermodynamic breakdowns (overall ΔG, duplex ΔG, target opening cost, etc.) for each candidate, offering advanced users insights into the driving forces behind the prediction. The web server also offers advanced options to adjust calculation rigor (e.g., folding method).
section: OligoWalk - Raw Article Summary / Advantages:
parent_content:
Tool Name: OligoWalk
Developer/Unit: Zhi John Lu and David H. Mathews (University of Rochester Medical Center, USA)

content:
High Computational Cost: The rigorous partition function calculations, especially for long mRNA targets, are computationally intensive and time-consuming, requiring submission to a job queue.
Primary for Synthetic siRNA: The algorithm is trained and optimized for predicting the efficacy of chemically synthesized siRNA duplexes. Its applicability to DNA vector-based siRNA (shRNA) design, where transcriptional context matters, is not explicitly addressed.

Tool Name: OligoWalk

content: Sequence Length Limit: The web server has a sequence input limit (10,000 nt) and a practical folding window limit (default 1000 nt centered on the binding site) for the partition function calculation to manage computational load, which may not capture long-range structural interactions.

Tool Name: OligoWalk

content:
Tool Name: OligoWalk
Developer/Unit: Zhi John Lu and David H. Mathews (University of Rochester Medical Center, USA)
section: OligoWalk - Raw Article Summary

content:
I. Introduction
The OligoWalk web server is constructured to predict efficient siRNA candidates for an mRNA sequence on the basis of the statistical mechanics of the siRNA-target interaction. The secondary structures of the oligomer and target mRNA are considered in the OligoWalk algorithm. In addition to the thermodynamic parameters, a number of local sequence features are also considered by the Support Vector Machine embedded in the server. Please see References for the details.
section: User Guide / Complexity for Novices: While the default settings are user-friendly, the full utility and interpretation of the advanced thermodynamic parameters require a deeper understanding of RNA biophysics, potentially making it less accessible for beginners compared to rule-based tools.

content:
The input options are restricted to save the calculation time on server. The complete software package without restrictions is available for download.
Oligomer Type: Only RNA is allowed.
Oligomer Length: 19 bases is recommended for siRNA design.
Oligomer Concentration: This is the oligomer concentration. It only affects overall ΔG that is provided as part of the thermodynamic details and it is not used for siRNA selection.
Prefilter for siRNA design: The prefiter is modified from Reynold's method. The prefilter only allows candidate siRNAs that meet some basic sequence criteria to be considered using the full thermodynamic model. It must be turned on when the secondary structure of the target is considered in order to save time.
section: II. Input Options / Complexity for Novices: While the default settings are user-friendly, the full utility and interpretation of the advanced thermodynamic parameters require a deeper understanding of RNA biophysics, potentially making it less accessible for beginners compared to rule-based tools.

content:
"Break local structure": The target will be folded once. The base pairs of the binding region are broken for the hybridization of the oligomer, but the structures of the other parts will not be refolded. This mode is a compromise between the fastest and most rigorous methods.
"Refold target RNA" : A certain range of region (the size is defined by Folding size, specified below) centered on the binding site will be refolded after oligomer hybridization. This mode is the most rigorous if it is assumed that the system is at equilibrium and this is the recommended mode for siRNA selection.
"Not consider target structure": not consider the secondary structure of the target. This is the fastest mode.
section: II. Input Options / Binding mode: Three modes are available(below).

content:
"Only consider the Optimal Structure": Only the lowest free energy structure is considered for the secondary structure prediction of RNA.
"Consider Suboptimal Structures": Suboptimal structures are also considered using a heuristic method to find suboptimal RNA secondary structures.
"Consider all possible structures using partition funcion": All possible structures are considered with a partition function calculation. This method is the most rigorous and is recommended for siRNA selection.
section: II. Input Options / Folding option: Three options are available(below).

content: Scan region: The positions on the target region to be scanned to find the candidates. By defining a sub-region of the sequence, considerable compute time can be saved.
section: II. Input Options / Folding size: When secondary structure of the taget is selected to be considered ("Break local structure" or "Refold target RNA"), the user can define the folding size of target. Then, a certain number of nucleotides centered at the binding region are considered for the secondary structure prediction.

content:
The server generates an siRNA candidate table ranked by the probability of being efficient at
knock-down. It also outputs a thermodynamic table:
1. Overall ΔG: The net free energy change when all contributions in the above figure are considered. The target-siRNA complex product prefers negative Overall ΔG .
2. Duplex ΔG : The free energy change of hybidized duplex between oligomer and target. Stable duplex has more negative ΔG than unstable one.
3. Tm-dup: The melting temperature in degrees C for the hybrided duplex formation.
4. Break-targ. ΔG: The free energy cost for opening base pairs in the region of complementarity to the target. More negative value means it is less accessible for siRNA binding.
section: II. Input Options / III. Output

content:
6. Interoligo ΔG: The free energy change of oligo-oligo dimer. Stable dimerization has more negative ΔG than unstable one.
7. Number of structures: The number of suboptimal structures of target used before and after the binding of oligomer. This is only available when "Consider Suboptimal Structures" is selected.
8. End-diff. : The free energy difference between the 5' and 3' end of the antisense strand of siRNA, with windows of 2. It has been shown that a functional siRNA prefer to have an unstable 5' end (End-diff is more positive).
9. Prefilter score: The score calculated using Reynold et al. method.
section: II. Input Options / 5. Intraoligo ΔG: The free energy change of the self structure of unimolecular oligo. Stable self structure has more negative ΔG than unstable one.

content:
Calculation time and memory size :
The benchmarks were performed with the default options:
The oligonucleotide was a 19 base RNA; the folding size of the target was 800 nucleotides centering on the binding site (full length if the whole target has less than 800 nt); the partition function calculation was conducted; the entire mRNA was scanned; the prefilter was turned on. The time cost is similar for long sequence because the prefitler (7) is turned on and number of candidates being folded is limited.
section: IV. Calculation Time / 5. Intraoligo ΔG: The free energy change of the self structure of unimolecular oligo. Stable self structure has more negative ΔG than unstable one.

content:
The caculation is tested on mRNA NM_020548 which is 730 nucleotides in length. The oligomer type is RNA. The oligomer length is 19 bases. The folding size is all, the full length of the mRNA. The scanned region size is from the first base to the end. There are 712 and 132 oligomer
candidates generated without and with prefilter turned on, respectively. The calculation time without prefilter is shown in parenthesis.
section: IV. Calculation Time / b The calculation time for sequence less than 800 nucleotides scales much less because some arrays are reused for such kind of calculation (25).

content:
The OligoWalk algorithm is based on the following research:
The efficient siRNA selection method is described in: Z.J. Lu and D.H. Mathews. (2008) "Efficient siRNA selection using hybridization thermodynamics" Nucleic Acids Research, 36:640-647.
The siRNA prefilter method is described in: Reynolds, A., Leake, D., Boese, Q., Scaringe, S., Marshall, W.S. and Khvorova, A. (2004) Rational siRNA design for RNA interference. Nat Biotechnol., 22, 326-330.
The thermodynamic parameters and algorithm of RNA secondary structure prediction are described in:
Lu, Z.J., Turner, D.H. and Mathews, D.H. (2006) A set of nearest neighbor parameters for predicting the enthalpy change of RNA secondary structure formation. Nucleic Acids Res., 34, 4912-4924.

section: V. References / Other research related to OligoWalk:

content:
·Target Site Accessibility as Primary Criterion:
Employs the RNAplfold program to compute the local accessibility of the target mRNA site, a factor often neglected in other design tools. This method uses a sliding window approach to calculate the probability that a stretch of nucleotides is unpaired, considering the full Boltzmann ensemble of possible local structures.
·Optimized Accessibility Regions:
Identifies two key lengths of unpaired regions that best correlate with siRNA efficacy: the seed region (8 nucleotides) at the 3' end of the target site and an extended region (16 nucleotides) critical for efficient RISC-mediated cleavage.
·Combination of Key Filters:
Integrates target accessibility with other biologically motivated design criteria:
Asymmetry: Ensures correct guide strand selection.
Self-folding & Free-end: Assesses the siRNA guide strand's own secondary structure.
·Hierarchical Ranking:
Instead of a combined score, candidates are ranked by their worst-performing criterion, ensuring that siRNAs with a critical flaw in any step of the RNAi pathway are deprioritized.

parent_content:
Tool Name: RNAxs
Developer/Unit: Hakim Tafer, Stefan L. Ameres, Gregor Obernosterer et al. (University of Vienna, Austrian Academy of Sciences, and other Austrian research institutions)

content:
Focus on Target Structure: Uniquely and rigorously incorporates target mRNA site accessibility as a central, validated design principle, addressing a downstream bottleneck in the RNAi mechanism.
Superior Performance: In independent validation (360 siRNAs), RNAxs outperformed six other design tools (including OligoWalk and commercial tools). All of its top three predicted siRNAs for four test genes achieved at least 50% repression, with many exceeding 90%.
Transparent & Biologically Grounded: Uses comprehensible filters based on distinct stages of the RNAi pathway (strand selection, siRNA structure, target binding), offering users adjustable parameters.
Provides a Full Candidate List: Returns a ranked list of all siRNAs passing the filters, allowing users to select sequences for specific needs (e.g., targeting splice variants).
section: RNAxs - Raw Article Summary / Advantages:
parent_content:
Tool Name: RNAxs
Developer/Unit: Hakim Tafer, Stefan L. Ameres, Gregor Obernosterer et al. (University of Vienna, Austrian Academy of Sciences, and other Austrian research institutions)

content:
Primary for Synthetic siRNA: The algorithm and validation are focused on chemically synthesized siRNA efficacy. Its direct applicability to DNA vector-based shRNA design is not explicitly addressed.
Local Folding Assumption: The accessibility calculation uses a local folding window (default 80nt), which may not capture the influence of very long-range mRNA secondary structures.
Parameter Sensitivity: Optimal parameters (like the critical unpaired region length u) differed between training datasets targeting 3' UTRs versus coding sequences, suggesting performance may vary by target region.
No Integrated Off-Target Scoring: While the web server allows manual BLAST submission, the core algorithm does not automatically score or rank candidates based on genome-wide specificity to minimize off-target effects.

Tool Name: RNAxs
Developer/Unit: Hakim Tafer, Stefan L. Ameres, Gregor Obernosterer et al. (University of Vienna, Austrian Academy of Sciences, and other Austrian research institutions)
section: RNAxs - Raw Article Summary

content:
I. Introduction
The RNAxs web server is designed to identify effective siRNA candidates by explicitly modeling the structural accessibility of target mRNA sites, a critical downstream determinant of RNA interference (RNAi) efficiency. Unlike many siRNA design tools that rely primarily on sequence composition or empirical scoring schemes, RNAxs evaluates whether a candidate siRNA can physically access and bind its target region within the folded mRNA. In addition to accessibility, the server incorporates biologically motivated constraints related to guide strand selection, siRNA structural stability, and duplex end properties. Together, these criteria reflect key checkpoints along the RNAi pathway and enable the selection of siRNAs with a high likelihood of functional silencing.
section: RNAxs Web Server User Guide / Limitations:

content:
Purpose and Usage
The sequence input field is used to provide the target RNA sequence(s) for siRNA design. RNAxs operates on RNA secondary structure models; therefore, the input sequence must correspond to a mature RNA transcript rather than genomic DNA.
Design Standards and Input Format
Accepted format: plain text or FASTA; Sequence orientation: 5′ → 3′; Maximum length: 20,000 nucleotides; Multiple FASTA entries may be provided to enable common or comparative targeting (e.g., splice variants or homologs).
Practical Guidance
Users should input full-length mRNA sequences whenever possible, including untranslated regions, as RNAxs will automatically evaluate all eligible target sites across the transcript.
section: RNAxs Web Server User Guide / II. Target Sequence Input

content:
8nt (Seed) Accessibility Threshold
Role and Biological Meaning
This parameter defines the minimum accessibility required for the 8-nucleotide seed region of the target site. Seed accessibility governs the initial recognition and binding of the siRNA guide strand by the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC).
Design Standards
A higher threshold enforces stricter requirements for local unpaired probability, reducing the number of candidate siRNAs but increasing confidence in initial binding efficiency.
Value Range and Reference Setting
Allowed range: 0–1 (floating-point number); Default value: approximately 0.01; Recommended usage: retain the default value for most applications; slight relaxation may be considered for highly structured targets.
section: RNAxs Web Server User Guide / Target Site Accessibility Parameters

content:
Role and Biological Meaning
The 16-nucleotide accessibility threshold represents the openness of an extended target region required for efficient RISC-mediated cleavage. This parameter is the central design criterion of RNAxs and the primary source of its predictive strength.
Design Standards
Because extended unpaired regions are relatively rare but highly predictive of siRNA efficacy, this threshold is intentionally stringent. Over-adjustment can severely reduce candidate yield.
Value Range and Reference Setting
Allowed range: 0–1 (floating-point number); Default value: approximately 0.001; Recommended usage: keep the default for standard siRNA design; minor relaxation may be acceptable when targeting 3′ UTR regions
section: RNAxs Web Server User Guide / 16nt Accessibility Threshold

content:
Self Folding Energy
Role and Biological Meaning
This parameter evaluates the tendency of the siRNA guide strand to form internal secondary structures, which may hinder RISC loading or target binding.
Design Standards
Higher values enforce stronger exclusion of siRNAs with self-folding potential and favor structurally simple guide strands.
Value Range and Reference Setting
Allowed range: 0–1 (floating-point number); Default value: approximately 0.9; Recommended usage: retain default for chemically synthesized siRNAs; modest relaxation may be considered for vector-based designs.
section: III. siRNA Structural Stability and Strand Selection / 16nt Accessibility Threshold

content:
Role and Biological Meaning
Sequence asymmetry reflects nucleotide composition differences between the two ends of the siRNA duplex, contributing to preferential guide strand selection.
Design Standards
This parameter implements established RNAi design principles and works in conjunction with energy asymmetry to bias RISC loading.
Value Range and Reference Setting
Allowed range: 0–1 (floating-point number); Default value: 0.5; Recommended usage: do not modify unless there is a specific experimental rationale.
section: III. siRNA Structural Stability and Strand Selection / Sequence Asymmetry

content:
Role and Biological Meaning
Energy asymmetry measures thermodynamic stability differences between the two ends of the siRNA duplex. A less stable 5′ end on the guide strand favors correct strand incorporation into RISC.
Design Standards
Moderate asymmetry is sufficient; overly strict thresholds may eliminate otherwise effective candidates.
Value Range and Reference Setting
Allowed range: 0–1 (floating-point number); Default value: approximately 0.46; Recommended usage: retain default in most scenarios
section: III. siRNA Structural Stability and Strand Selection / Energy Asymmetry

content:
Role and Biological Meaning
The free end parameter assesses whether the terminal nucleotides of the siRNA duplex are excessively base-paired. Open ends facilitate RISC recognition and processing.
Design Standards
Higher values require greater terminal openness but may reduce candidate numbers if set too strictly.
Value Range and Reference Setting
Allowed range: 0–1 (floating-point number); Default value: approximately 0.62; Recommended usage: default setting is appropriate for standard siRNA experiments
section: III. siRNA Structural Stability and Strand Selection / Free End

content:
Role and Intended Use
Custom sequence rules allow users to impose explicit nucleotide-level constraints on candidate siRNAs for technical or experimental reasons, such as synthesis compatibility or cloning requirements.
Design Standards
This option should be used conservatively and must not be employed to replace or override RNAxs’s accessibility-based decision logic.
Input Format and Reference Setting
Input format: exactly 19 characters, one per nucleotide position
Allowed characters: standard IUPAC nucleotide codes (e.g., A, U, G, C, N)
Default value: NNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNNN (no constraints)
section: IV. Custom Sequence Rules / Free End

content:
Maximal Number of siRNAs
Role and Usage
This parameter controls how many top-ranked siRNA candidates are displayed in the output. It does not influence scoring or ranking, only result presentation.
Value Range and Reference Setting
Input type: positive integer; Default value: 3; Recommended usage: 3–5 for routine experiments; higher values for exploratory analysis
section: IV. Custom Sequence Rules / V. Output Control

content: In routine use, RNAxs achieves optimal performance when default parameter values are preserved, particularly for accessibility thresholds. Parameter modification should be minimal, biologically motivated, and applied only when the experimental context clearly justifies deviation. When used in this manner, RNAxs provides a robust and mechanistically grounded framework for high-confidence siRNA selection.
section: IV. Custom Sequence Rules / Practical Design Principle

·Statistical Sampling Algorithm:
Employs a novel algorithm to sample rigorously from the Boltzmann ensemble of RNA secondary structures, providing a statistically representative set of probable structures, which is crucial for modeling the structural population of mRNAs.

content:
Target Accessibility: Uses a weighted antisense siRNA binding energy, incorporating local unpaired probabilities from the ensemble.
Empirical Rules:Implements rules (e.g., from Reynolds et al.) for sequence features and GC content.
siRNA Duplex Thermodynamics: Calculates stability indices for duplex asymmetry (DSSE) and cleavage site instability (AIS), integrating findings from contemporary research.
Multi-Application Suite: Provides specialized modules for different RNA-targeting nucleic acids: Sima (siRNA), Soligo (antisense oligonucleotides), Sribo (hammerhead ribozymes), and Sma (general statistical folding analysis).
section: Sfold - Raw Article Summary / ·Combines three key aspects for rational siRNA design:
parent_content:
Tool Name: Sfold
Developer/Unit: Ye Ding, Chi Yu Chan, and Charles E. Lawrence (Wadsworth Center, New York State Department of Health, USA)

content:
Ensemble-Based Accessibility: Pioneered the use of a statistical ensemble approach to predict target site accessibility, moving beyond single minimum free energy structures to better reflect the dynamic nature of mRNA.
Comprehensive Design Integration: One of the earliest tools to systematically combine target accessibility prediction with empirical rules and detailed thermodynamic profiling of the siRNA duplex itself.
Versatility: Offers a unified platform for the design of multiple types of RNA-targeting agents (siRNA, antisense oligos, ribozymes), not just siRNAs.
Transparent Scoring: Provides a detailed total score (max 20) broken down into accessibility, duplex feature, and thermodynamics components, allowing users to understand the basis of the recommendation.
section: Sfold - Raw Article Summary / Advantages:
parent_content:
Tool Name: Sfold
Developer/Unit: Ye Ding, Chi Yu Chan, and Charles E. Lawrence (Wadsworth Center, New York State Department of Health, USA)

content:
Dated Algorithm: As a 2004 tool, its core sampling algorithm and the empirical/thermodynamic rules it uses may not incorporate subsequent major advances in siRNA design principles discovered in later years.
Computational Intensity: The statistical sampling approach, while rigorous, is computationally more demanding than simpler rule-based methods of its era.
Lack of Machine Learning: Unlike later "second-generation" tools (e.g., i-Score, OligoWalk), it does not employ machine learning models trained on large siRNA efficacy datasets to optimize predictions.
Potential Over-reliance on Fixed Rules: The filtering system is based on fixed thresholds for parameters like binding energy and AIS, which may not be optimal for all targets or experimental contexts.

content:
Tool Name: Sfold
Developer/Unit: Ye Ding, Chi Yu Chan, and Charles E. Lawrence (Wadsworth Center, New York State Department of Health, USA)
section: Sfold - Raw Article Summary

content:
I. FUNCTION
Sfold predicts probable RNA secondary structures, assesses target accessibility, and provides tools for the rational design of RNA-targeting nucleic acids.
section: Sfold Web Server User Guide / Limitations:

content:
RNA folding and applications
Sfold is based on patent-pending algorithms developed by Ding and Lawrence (2001, 2002, 2003) for RNA folding, prediction of target accessibility, and rational design of RNA-targeting nucleic acids. The RNA folding algorithm generates a statistical sample of secondary structures from the Boltzmann ensemble of RNA secondary structures. From a statistical mechanics perspective, an RNA molecule can have a population of structures distributed according to a Boltzmann distribution, which gives the probability of a secondary structure I at equilibrium as (1/U)exp[-E(I)/RT], where E(I) is the free energy of the structure, R is the gas constant, T is the absolute temperature, and U is the partition function for all admissible secondary structures of the RNA sequence. The algorithm samples secondary structures exactly and rigorously according to the Boltzmann distribution, using recent Turner free energy rules.
section: II. DESCRIPTION / Limitations:

Rules for siRNA design and Sfold design methodology / A focus of Sfold is on applications of prediction of target accessibility, and rational design of RNA-targeting nucleic acids. Three modules, Sirna, Soligo and Sribo, provide application tools for the design of shortinterfering RNAs (siRNAs), antisense oligonucleotides (oligos), and trans-cleaving ribozymes, respectively.General folding features and output are available from the fourth module, Srna.

content: Sfold is available through web server at two sites: http://sfold.wadsworth.org/ and http://www.bioinfo.rpi.edu/applications/sfold/. On the server home page, a user can select the application module of primary interest. From the output page for the selected module, links for output are provided for the other modules, with default input settings as described below.
section: III. INPUT / A note to all users: please do not design siRNAs or other RNA-targeting nucleic acids based on a single structure from module Srna. We believe that there may be an ensemble of structures for mRNAs and viral RNAs. Please refer to FAQ # 3 on Sfold front page.

content:
Folding conditions. Folding temperature is 37 o C. Ionic conditions are 1M NaCl, with no divalent ions.
Job mode and limits. A job can run in either interactive mode or batch mode. Current limits are 200 bases for an interactive job, and 5,000 bases for a batch job. For a batch job, a correct e-mail address is required, for notification of job completion.
For a sequence exceeding the length limit, the user needs to truncate the sequence for folding. It is quite possible that some portions of the secondary structure of a truncated sequence will be different from the structure of the full sequence. This in turn can change the predicted accessibility of some sites on the target.
section: III. INPUT / Common information for all modules

content: Centering: For a decision on where to truncate the sequence, we suggest that the region of primary interest be located far way from the sites of the truncation, e.g., near the center of the truncated sequence. Our computational experience suggests that the accessibility of a site is more likely to be maintained when the site is located near the center of the truncated sequence. This can be explained by the fact that, to a great extent, RNA secondary structure is determined by nearest-neighbor interactions. The regions near the 5' and 3' ends of the shortened sequence, i.e., the sites of truncation, are most likely to have different predictions for local structures and accessibility.
section: III. INPUT / To computationally address this issue, we suggest two strategies:

content:
Sirna: The length of siRNAs with 3' TT overhangs is fixed at 21 nt.
Soligo: The user has the option to set the length of the oligos. The default length is 20 nt.
Sribo: The user can specify an NUH cleavage triplet for hammerhead ribozymes. The default triplet is GUC.
Srna: None.
section: III. INPUT / Module-specific input information

content: The output page for each module includes both graphical representations and relevant text files. Many of the graphics are interactive through a web browser, and the user can download a colored or black and white plot in PDF or PostScript format. For monochromic printers, the black and white version is recommended. All of the graphs, with the exception of RNA structure diagrams, are first generated by the public domain software Gnuplot and then post-processed by Perl scripts to provide additional features and to improve visualization. Links to output from other modules with default input settings are provided. Output can be accessed on the server for up to 72 hours after job completion.
section: IV. OUTPUT / Module-specific input information

content:
Graphical output
Probability profiling for visual display of accessibility prediction. For prediction of target accessibility, a complete probability profile of single-stranded regions is generated for the entire target RNA. Sites with high probabilities of being single-stranded are predicted to be accessible. At nucleotide position i, the profile shows the probability that nucleotides i, i+1, i+2, and i+3 are all unpaired. In other words, the profile is for consecutive fragments with a width W of 4 bases. Although the profile can be generated for any W, W=4 has been found to be particularly useful (Ding & Lawrence 2001). Currently, colored or black and white profile plots are available in PDF and PostScript formats.
section: V. Sirna / Module-specific input information

V. Sirna / For RNA sequences longer than 200 bases, the regional probability profile allows the user to examine either any region of 200 bases, by specifying the starting position, or an adjacent region of 200 bases, by clicking

content: Implementation of target accessibility rule: The probability profile displays predicted accessible sites on the target RNA. Because an accessible site can be targeted by a number of siRNAs, selection of the "optimal" one can be based on binding energy of the antisense strand, together with other design rules. Stronger binding is indicated by smaller binding energy (stacking energies are negatively valued). For example, an antisense siRNA with a binding energy of -15 kcal/mol is predicted to be more effective than an antisensesiRNA with a binding energy of -10 kcal/mol. The antisense siRNA binding energy is a weighted sum of the RNA/RNA stacking energies (Xia et al. 1998) for the hybrid formed by the antisense siRNA and the targeted sequence. For a base-pair stack, the weight for the sum is calculated by the probability of the unpaired dinucleotide in the target sequence that is involved in the stack. In addition, A-U terminal penalty is included and is weighted by the probability of the unpaired terminal base. This weighting scheme accounts for the structural variation at the target site. The target accessibility rule is implemented by requiring the siRNA binding energy to be below a threshold value. The current default of the threshold is -10 kcal/mol.
section: Text output files / siRNA internal stability profiling: The internal stability profile for every possible siRNA duplex is available through the interactive graphic window for siRNA ends and internal stability profiling. On the profile, the user can use the stabilities for positions 1 and 19 to make a visual comparison of the stabilities of siRNA duplex ends. The stability profiles are not included in the Zip or tar.gz file for Sfold output, because of large number of profiles for long mRNAs. However, from the interactive profiling window, the user can download the profiles for the selected target sites.

content:
End-to-End Commercial Integration & Reliability: The tool is seamlessly embedded within IDT's industry-leading oligonucleotide synthesis and ordering platform. Designed sequences (both standard siRNA and advanced DsiRNA) can be ordered immediately with guaranteed quality, offering an unmatched, low-risk workflow from design to experiment.
Specialization in High-Potency Formats: It uniquely provides optimized design for Dicer-Substrate RNAs (DsiRNAs), which are reported to offer significantly increased potency and durability of silencing compared to standard 21-mer siRNAs, a feature not commonly found in free academic tools.
Curated, Multi-Rule Synthesis with BLAST Specificity Check: The algorithm synthesizes parameters from several major, experimentally-derived guidelines, potentially increasing robustness. The web interface integrates BLAST analysis against target species genomes to filter for specificity, a critical step for reducing off-target effects.
Vendor-Optimized and Supported: The designs are tuned for performance with IDT's proprietary chemistries and are backed by extensive internal validation and technical support, reducing experimental uncertainty.
section: IDT DsiRNA Design Tool - Summary / Advantages:
parent_content:
Tool Name: IDT RNAi Design Tool (including Dicer-Substrate siRNA / DsiRNA design)
Developer / Unit: Integrated DNA Technologies (IDT)
Core Principles:
·Unified Rule Integration for Standard siRNA: Combines key design criteria from multiple seminal publications (Elbashir, Reynolds, Ui-Tei, Amarzguioui, Hsieh) into a single algorithm. This includes the AA-N19 motif, avoidance of homopolymer runs, position-specific nucleotide preferences, and enforcement of thermodynamic asymmetry to promote antisense strand RISC loading.
·Specialized Dicer-Substrate RNA (DsiRNA) Design: Offers a distinct, advanced algorithm for designing 27-mer duplexes (DsiRNAs) based on the principle that providing a substrate for the Dicer enzyme, rather than its product (21-mer siRNA), can enhance potency. The design ensures the predicted Dicer cleavage product corresponds to an effective 21-mer core, incorporating asymmetric overhangs and specific chemical modifications (e.g., DNA bases) to direct processing.

content:
Proprietary and Non-Transparent Algorithm: The exact weighting, implementation, and validation datasets for both its standard siRNA and DsiRNA algorithms are not publicly disclosed. This "black-box" nature limits independent assessment and direct comparison with transparent, peer-reviewed tools like OligoWalk.
Primarily Rule-Based, Lacks Advanced In Silico Predictive Modeling: While incorporating key rules, the tool does not appear to implement sophisticated computational models such as target mRNA accessibility calculations via partition function (a core strength of OligoWalk) or machine learning efficacy predictors that consider the full thermodynamic ensemble.
Scope and Flexibility: The tool is primarily optimized for designing synthetic siRNA/DsiRNA duplexes for direct purchase from IDT. It offers less flexibility for specialized applications that require extensive parameter customization, integration with custom vector backbones for shRNA (unlike siRNA Wizard), or for researchers who do not plan to use IDT as their synthesis provider.

section: IDT DsiRNA Design Tool - Summary

content: The rational selection of effective RNAi molecules for gene knockdown involves including features that enhance the success and avoiding features that increase the failure of the resulting RNAi molecule. Software for the design of effective RNAi molecules should select candidate RNAi sequences based upon their ability to conform to effectiveness criteria. IDT’s default unified RNAi design parameters incorporate the following criteria for RNAi selection and ranking from the target sequence of interest.

content:
Early, Rule-Integrated Pioneer: As one of the first dedicated web servers, it systematically integrated the early, foundational design rules (Tuschl patterns, thermodynamic asymmetry) and filtering criteria into an accessible, automated platform.
Comprehensive Specificity Check: Its integrated BLAST against UniGene and genomic mapping (for SNPs, exon junctions) provided a more thorough specificity analysis for its time compared to simple rule-based filtering.
Flexibility and Transparency: Offers users significant control over the initial sequence pattern and filter parameters, and presents clear thermodynamic calculations and specificity annotations, making the selection process transparent.
section: Whitehead siRNA Selection Server - Raw Article Summary / Advantages:
parent_content:
Tool Name: Whitehead siRNA (short interfering RNA) Selection Web Server
Developer / Unit: Whitehead Institute for Biomedical Research (Collaboration with Tuschl lab)
Core Principles:
·Identifies candidate siRNA sequences (21mers) by applying user-selectable sequence patterns (e.g., AAN19TT) and composition filters (GC content, exclusion of homopolymer runs).
Calculates and reports the thermodynamic stability difference between the 5' ends of the sense and antisense strands, favoring designs where the antisense 5' end is less stable to promote its entry into RISC.
·Performs automated BLAST searches against human or mouse UniGene databases to assess sequence uniqueness and minimize off-target effects, and maps candidates to genomes to highlight exon-exon boundaries and SNP locations.

content:
Outdated Algorithmic Foundation: Relies solely on early-generation sequence patterns and simple thermodynamic end-stability calculations. It lacks the sophisticated thermodynamic models (e.g., partition function for target accessibility) or machine learning classifiers found in later tools like OligoWalk.
No Validated Efficacy Prediction: Unlike tools like OligoWalk (78.6% PPV) or commercial platforms linked to validated reagents, this server does not provide a quantitative, experimentally-validated probability score for silencing efficacy; ranking is based on basic rules and user sorting.
Limited Scope and Features: Designed primarily for synthetic siRNA design. It does not support shRNA design features (like loop insertion) as seen in tools like siRNA Wizard, and its functionality is confined to the rule sets available around 2004.

section: Whitehead siRNA Selection Server - Raw Article Summary

content:
·Linear Regression Model:
Developed by applying a linear regression model to a large dataset of 2431 siRNAs to determine nucleotide preference scores at each position of a 19-nt siRNA duplex.
Position-Specific Scoring: The algorithm assigns scores based solely on which nucleotide (A, U, G, C) appears at each of the 19 positions, reflecting empirically observed preferences for effective siRNAs.
·Thermodynamic Stability Filter:
A key finding is that siRNA duplex thermodynamic stability, represented by the whole ΔG value (stacking energy), is critical for reliable prediction. The study recommends excluding highly thermostable siRNAs (whole ΔG < -34.6 kcal/mol) to significantly improve prediction accuracy.
·Validation System:
The team developed a dual-vector experimental validation system (pSELL target vector + pDual effector vector) to conveniently test predicted siRNAs.
section: i-Score - Raw Article Summary / Core Principles:
parent_content:
Tool Name: i-Score (inhibitory-Score)
Developer/Unit: Masatoshi Ichihara et al. (Chubu University & Nagoya University Graduate School of Medicine, Japan)

content:
Simplicity & Transparency: Algorithm is straightforward, based only on position-specific nucleotide scores, making it easy to understand and trace compared to "black-box" neural network models (e.g., Biopredsi).
High Yield of Predictions: At a ~90% success rate threshold, i-Score can predict at least 8 times more potentially active siRNA candidates than first-generation algorithms (e.g., Reynolds, Katoh).
Comparable Accuracy: Prediction accuracy (correlation with experimental data) is as good as other contemporary second-generation algorithms (s-Biopredsi, ThermoComposition21, DSIR).
Integrated Designer Tool: The provided i-Score designer (Excel VBA) calculates scores for 11 different siRNA design algorithms and key parameters (like whole ΔG), allowing cross-comparison within one platform.
Universal Validation Method: The pSELL/pDual vector system offers a cost-effective and flexible way to experimentally validate siRNA activity for any sequence.
section: i-Score - Raw Article Summary / Advantages:
parent_content:
Tool Name: i-Score (inhibitory-Score)
Developer/Unit: Masatoshi Ichihara et al. (Chubu University & Nagoya University Graduate School of Medicine, Japan)

content:
Primarily for Synthetic siRNA: The algorithm is trained and optimized for chemically synthesized siRNA duplexes, not specifically for DNA vector-based siRNA or siRNA cassette design.
Limited by Thermodynamic Stability: The algorithm cannot accurately predict the activity of highly thermostable siRNA duplexes (low whole ΔG), which represents a class of siRNAs where current design rules fail.
Potential for Overfitting: Like DSIR, i-Score's performance on its training dataset (Dataset A) is better than on independent validation sets, suggesting possible overfitting to the specific dataset used for development.
Model Simplicity: Relying only on positional nucleotide preferences may overlook other complex sequence or structural features that influence siRNA efficacy.

section: siDesign center - Raw Article Summary

content:
0. Introduction
The siDESIGN Center is an advanced, user-friendly tool for designing potent custom siRNAs. It employs an algorithm that considers various design criteria, such as avoiding motifs linked to toxicity, known microRNA seed sequences, and polymorphisms. Additionally, it offers advanced design options to discriminate transcript variants or add transcript targets from another gene or a different species.
Please note that the custom siRNAs designed with the siDESIGN Center are not functionally guaranteed. However, siRNAs designed using this tool are more likely to provide potent and specific knockdown than those designed with conventional methods.
section: siDesign center user guide / Limitations:

content:
The siDESIGN Center offers three options for identifying the target sequence of interest.
A. Accession Number
Accession numbers can be found in the NCBI Reference Sequence (RefSeq) database (link). This is a non-redundant, curated, and annotated collection of sequence records for major model organisms. RefSeq accession numbers use the systematic naming convention below.
section: Step 1: Choose an Identifier Type / Limitations:

content:
The Nucleotide box accepts FASTA-formatted sequence data up to 10,000 nucleotides. Only mature RNA or cDNA sequence should be entered. Sequences should be formatted using standard nucleic acid codes: A, C, G, T, and U. Any other alphanumeric entries will be ignored. FASTA format requires a ">" symbol and a line break prior to entering or pasting the nucleotide sequence. A name may also be designated following the “>”symbol and before the page break, as shown below:
>SGene 4582 Conserved Region
GACTAGCATGCAGCATGCAGAGCTAGCAGCGAGCAGCAGCATGCATGCAGCAGCAGCATCGAGCAGCAGCATGCATGCAGCAGCAGCAGCAGCAGCAGCATGAGAGCAGCAGCAGCCTAGCTAGCATGCATGCATGCATGCAGCATGCATGCATGCATGCTAGCTAGCTAGTCGATGCATGCATGCAGCGACGATATCGATTACAGCGACGATGCATGCATGCATGCAGCATGCATGCATGATGATATAT
section: Step 1: Choose an Identifier Type / B. Nucleotide Sequence

content:
This is the unique ID number assigned to each gene in the NCBI database, as you can find, for
instance, on our website:
672 (BRCA1)
25 (ABL1)
983 (CDC2)
6419 (SETMAR)
section: Step 1: Choose an Identifier Type / C. Gene ID

content:
A series of digits that are assigned consecutively to each sequence record processed by NCBI. The GI number bears no resemblance to the Accession number of the sequence record. If a sequence changes in any way, a new GI number will be assigned. Be sure the GI Number provided is for the nucleotide, not the protein sequence of interest.
Example: 27436944 (Homo sapiens lamin A/C (LMNA), transcript variant 2, mRNA)
section: Step 1: Choose an Identifier Type / D. GI Number (GenInfo Identifier)

content:
It is important to note that the BLAST option (Step 4) must be checked and the proper species database selected, otherwise our tool will not take into consideration any advanced options.
a. Selection of accessions
The advanced options support more complex design requirements, such as isoform-specific siRNA designs. However, using these options can greatly reduce the number of siRNA designs generated due to the increased stringency of the design parameters.
When the Accession number or Gene ID are entered in Step 1, selecting the Advanced Settings will display the additional accession numbers for all RefSeq variants associated with the target gene. As a default, all variants will be “required” except non-coding accessions, which will be denoted with the “NR” accession prefix.
section: Advanced Options / D. GI Number (GenInfo Identifier)

content:
Select "Allowed" for any transcript variant that will neither be required nor excluded by resulting siRNA designs.
Select "Excluded" for any transcript variant whose sequence should not be targeted. Use of Advanced Options to exclude a variant will require selection of a BLAST database in Step 4.
section: Advanced Options / Select "Required" for any transcript variant that must be targeted by the siRNA designs. Note that at least one variant must remain "Required" for the design step to proceed.

content:
This option supports the design of siRNAs that target multiple genes with homologous regions. For example, homologous genes across multiple species, or conserved gene family members within a species. Using this option may greatly reduce the number of siRNA designs generated due to the increased stringency of the design parameters.
Up to 10 Accession numbers may be entered in the field provided. These must be separated by spaces or line breaks. Gene targets entered here will be "Required" by the design tool along with the original targeted accessions selected.
section: Advanced Options / b. Additional required targets

content:
This step allows the user to specify which of the following regions to target:
5' UTR: check this box to include the 5' untranslated region (UTR) for design consideration
ORF: check this box to include designs that target the Open Reading Frame or ORF. This is the default selection. Non-coding RNAs will be treated as an ORF region and no designs will be returned if ORF is not selected. Recommended.
3' UTR: Check this box to include the 3' untranslated region (UTR). Recommended.
If nucleotide sequence was used for the input type in step 1, the sequence will be treated as the
ORF and selecting 5’UTR and 3’UTR will not have any impact.
$
Step 3: GC content
This value is calculated using the core 19 nucleotide target sequence and does not include the dinucleotide overhangs. Early research suggested that siRNA should have a G/C content of approximately 50%. For genes that are G/C poor or rich, the range of G/C content be expanded to include other ranges. The default and recommended range is between 30% and 64% GC content.
section: Step 2: Select desired regions for siRNA design / b. Additional required targets

content:
The siDESIGN Center offers the option to BLAST the target sequences against one or two species in order to remove the designs which have significant risk of presenting cross-targets.
No BLAST: Choose if there is no BLAST database available through this tool for the desired species, if specificity is less of a concern compared to functionality, or if you have entered a nucleotide sequence (at Step1) which is present in the targeted organism. (In this case, selecting “no BLAST” but doing a BLAST analysis on the siRNA designs through NCBI is recommended.)
BLAST: Select this option to perform a BLAST analysis to ensure specificity of the siRNA candidates and/or because you have entered advanced options. Select the host species from the drop-down menu.
section: Step 4: Choose BLAST Options / b. Additional required targets

content:
Note that 3 databases encompass non-coding transcripts in addition to protein-coding transcripts. These are available for Human, Mouse and Rat. You may select those if you wish to exclude some non-coding variants (Step1, advanced options) or if you are concerned about targeting any non-coding transcript.
Two combined databases are also available to support cross-species designs:
Combined Human/Mouse, Combined Human/Rat
Please use these databases if you have added transcripts from a different species with the advanced
options of Step 1.
If your species does not appear in this list, we recommend performing a BLAST analysis at NCBI with the candidate siRNAs target sequences. The siRNA sequences can be found by adding the siRNA designs to your cart and then selecting “More” from the line item in the cart.
section: Step 4: Choose BLAST Options / The following species-specific protein-coding transcript databases (RefSeq) are available for BLAST analyses: Human (H. sapiens), Mouse (M. musculus), Rat (R. norvegicus), Zebrafish (D. rerio), Fruitfly (D. melanogaster), Worm (C. elegans), Cow (B. taurus), Rhesus monkey (M. mulatta), Chimpanzee (P. troglodytes), Dog (C. lupus familiaris), Chicken (G. gallus), Chinese Hamster (C. griseus)

content:
A list of up to 50 of the top-scoring candidates is presented along with details which will help you select the best siRNAs. The candidates are sorted by the score. Here are explanations of each column:
Select: Click to choose siRNA designs that will be added to your shopping cart. When items are in your cart, be sure to click on“Edit”to select additional options, such as the type of siRNA, required processing, and desired quantity.
Region: Region where the design aligns to one of the indicated targeted transcripts. This field will
indicate "N/A" if the targeted gene is a non-protein-coding gene or if a nucleotide sequence was provided as the Identifier Type in Step 1.
Start Position: Position of the siRNA design relative to transcription starting site of one of the
indicated targeted transcripts.
GC% : Percentage of the 19-base target sequence that is G or C.
Score: Algorithmic indicator of the likelihood of successful silencing with this siRNA design. This score does not quantify expected percent knockdown but is a prediction based on characteristics of the sequence.
section: Results / The following species-specific protein-coding transcript databases (RefSeq) are available for BLAST analyses: Human (H. sapiens), Mouse (M. musculus), Rat (R. norvegicus), Zebrafish (D. rerio), Fruitfly (D. melanogaster), Worm (C. elegans), Cow (B. taurus), Rhesus monkey (M. mulatta), Chimpanzee (P. troglodytes), Dog (C. lupus familiaris), Chicken (G. gallus), Chinese Hamster (C. griseus)

content:
Min # mismatches (Antisense): The lowest number of mismatches of the 19-base antisense strand to a sequence record other than the required targets, as determined by BLAST analysis. For example, a score of 3 in this field would indicate that the antisense strand has no more than a 16/19 base match to any non-required transcript accession in the BLAST database.
Min # mismatches (Sense): Similar to above but for the sense strand.
section: Results / Low Seed Frequency: Yes/No indicator of whether the target sequence has a low seed sequence frequency within the 3'UTR of the target or host genome. siRNA with low frequency seed regions will have reduced risk of off-target effects relative to higher frequencies 1. When possible, choosing target sequences with a “Yes” here is recommended.

content:
Start over: this will clear the input so that new design parameters can be entered.
Modify: this will go back to the design steps with the original design parameters pre-filled to allow adjustments.
section: Results / Requires Sense Modification: This is a yes/no indicator of whether the siRNA design should be synthesized with a chemical modification to inhibit sense-strand uptake. This requirement is indicated when (a) The sense strand has fewer than 2 mismatches to non-targeted sequence record in the BLAST database or (b) analysis indicates that the design favors sense strand over antisense strand entry into RISC. If "No BLAST" was selected, only criteria (b) will be applied.
